tradeoffs.scenario: An industrial fastener distributor carries 28,000 SKUs across 14 product categories. Analysis reveals that 4,200 SKUs (15%) generate 82% of revenue. The remaining 23,800 SKUs have average inventory turns of 1.1x and occupy 55% of warehouse space. However, key accounts responsible for 45% of revenue require single-source purchasing — they will switch to competitors who stock the full range rather than place orders with multiple suppliers.

objective_a.label: Maintain broad product portfolio for market coverage

objective_b.label: Focus on high-performing SKUs per Pareto principle

tradeoffs.correct_strategy: Maintain the full 28,000-SKU catalog for key accounts but implement a tiered stocking model: top 4,200 SKUs stocked locally for immediate availability, next 8,000 held at a central DC with 2-day fulfillment, and the remaining 15,800 available as vendor-direct drop-ship with 5-7 day lead times
tradeoffs.correct_sacrifice: Slower fulfillment on long-tail SKUs means some key account orders take 5-7 days for specialty items, requiring account managers to set expectations and potentially losing urgency-driven purchases on rare items
tradeoffs.explanation: The Pareto principle suggests cutting the tail, but the single-source purchasing requirement means the tail protects the head — losing key accounts over missing SKUs would destroy 45% of revenue. The tiered stocking model applies Pareto logic to inventory investment rather than portfolio breadth. The top 15% of SKUs get premium stocking, while the long tail exists in the catalog without consuming warehouse space. This preserves the one-stop-shop value proposition while concentrating 85% of inventory capital on high-velocity products.

sources.title: The Art of Product Portfolio Management

distractor_strategies.strategy: Eliminate all 23,800 low-performing SKUs immediately and reinvest the freed warehouse space and capital into deeper stock of the top 4,200 SKUs with guaranteed same-day shipping
distractor_strategies.sacrifice: Key accounts requiring single-source purchasing defect to full-range competitors, putting 45% of revenue at risk

distractor_strategies.strategy: Keep all 28,000 SKUs fully stocked at every warehouse location and negotiate extended payment terms with suppliers to offset the working capital impact of slow-moving inventory
distractor_strategies.sacrifice: Extended payment terms strain supplier relationships and the 1.1x inventory turns on 55% of warehouse space remain an unaddressed drag on profitability

distractor_strategies.strategy: Spin off the long-tail SKUs into a separate online-only business unit with its own warehouse, keeping the core distribution business focused on high-velocity products
distractor_strategies.sacrifice: Key accounts now must order from two entities, defeating the single-source requirement and adding operational complexity without reducing total inventory

tradeoffs.scenario: A medical device company sells 3,400 SKUs, of which 620 are classified as slow-moving with fewer than 10 units sold per quarter. These slow movers consume $4.2M in annual carrying costs and 30% of warehouse capacity. However, 180 of these slow-moving SKUs are legacy surgical instruments still used by 15% of hospitals that haven't upgraded to newer systems, and discontinuing them would breach existing supply agreements with 3-5 year terms.

objective_a.label: Retain slow-moving products for customer completeness

objective_b.label: Aggressively rationalize SKUs to minimize inventory costs

tradeoffs.correct_strategy: Segment the 620 slow movers into three categories: (1) 180 contractually obligated SKUs maintained with minimum safety stock and made-to-order replenishment, (2) 200 SKUs with viable upgrade paths actively transitioned via hospital conversion programs with trade-in incentives, and (3) 240 truly obsolete SKUs with no contractual obligations discontinued with 12-month last-time-buy notices
tradeoffs.correct_sacrifice: The conversion program requires $800K in trade-in subsidies and dedicated sales resources for 18 months, and contractually obligated SKUs retain $1.4M in carrying costs that cannot be eliminated until agreements expire
tradeoffs.explanation: Blanket rationalization ignores the contractual and clinical realities of medical device supply chains. The segmented approach applies different obsolescence strategies based on actual constraints: contractual obligations get minimum-viable stocking, convertible products get active phase-out support, and truly dead SKUs get discontinued. This captures roughly 60% of the potential inventory savings ($2.5M of $4.2M) while protecting hospital relationships and honoring supply agreements. The conversion program accelerates natural obsolescence rather than waiting for contracts to expire.

distractor_strategies.strategy: Discontinue all 620 slow-moving SKUs simultaneously and offer hospitals a 20% discount on upgraded replacements to accelerate transition
distractor_strategies.sacrifice: Breach of supply agreements with 15% of hospitals triggers contractual penalties and potential loss of entire hospital system accounts

distractor_strategies.strategy: Maintain all 620 slow-moving SKUs but move them to a third-party consignment warehouse where carrying costs are shared with the warehouse operator
distractor_strategies.sacrifice: Consignment shifts storage costs but doesn't address the root problem of stocking products with declining demand, and the 3PL charges premium rates for low-volume handling

distractor_strategies.strategy: Increase minimum order quantities for all slow-moving SKUs to 50 units to force hospitals to buy in bulk or stop ordering, effectively pricing out low-demand products
distractor_strategies.sacrifice: Hospitals refuse bulk purchases of slow-moving items they rarely need, creating stockout situations that damage clinical relationships and violate supply commitments

tradeoffs.scenario: A consumer electronics accessories company launches 40-50 new products per year to capture emerging trends (wireless charging, MagSafe, USB-C transitions). Each launch requires 8 weeks of supply chain setup, $35K in initial inventory investment, and integration into 6 distribution channels. Currently, 30% of new launches fail to reach break-even within 12 months, and the constant churn creates forecasting errors that increase safety stock across the entire portfolio by 20%.

objective_a.label: Pursue rapid innovation with frequent new product launches

objective_b.label: Maintain portfolio stability and forecasting accuracy

tradeoffs.correct_strategy: Implement a stage-gate launch process: test all new products through a single direct-to-consumer channel first with minimal inventory ($8K), promote to full 6-channel distribution only after 90-day sell-through validation, and enforce a one-in-one-out rule where each new product promoted to full distribution requires retiring a bottom-performing existing SKU
tradeoffs.correct_sacrifice: Slower time-to-market for new products — the 90-day validation window means competitors may capture early adopters, and the one-in-one-out rule limits total portfolio size even when new products succeed
tradeoffs.explanation: The 30% failure rate at $35K per launch means the company wastes roughly $525K annually on failed products while the portfolio churn inflates safety stock costs across all SKUs. The stage-gate approach preserves innovation speed (still launching 40-50 products for testing) but gates the expensive supply chain integration behind proven demand. The one-in-one-out rule prevents portfolio bloat that degrades forecasting accuracy. The sacrifice is real — competitors may beat them to scale on trending products — but the 90-day window filters out the 30% of launches that would have failed anyway, saving both direct launch costs and the indirect forecasting penalty.

sources.title: Supply Chain Readiness for New Product Launches

distractor_strategies.strategy: Reduce new product launches from 40-50 to 10 per year, selecting only products with guaranteed demand based on pre-orders, and invest the saved resources into deeper inventory of proven best-sellers
distractor_strategies.sacrifice: Missing 75% of trend-driven opportunities in a fast-moving accessories market where first-mover advantage determines category winners

distractor_strategies.strategy: Increase launch velocity to 80+ products per year but reduce initial inventory investment to $15K per SKU and accept stockouts on successful products as a signal to scale up
distractor_strategies.sacrifice: Doubling the launch rate doubles forecasting chaos and the 20% safety stock penalty compounds, while stockouts on winning products hand market share to competitors at the critical growth moment

distractor_strategies.strategy: Launch all 40-50 products simultaneously across all 6 channels but use AI-driven demand sensing to dynamically adjust inventory levels in real time, eliminating the need for safety stock buffers
distractor_strategies.sacrifice: AI demand sensing cannot accurately forecast genuinely new products with no sales history, and full 6-channel launches still cost $35K each for the 30% that fail
